topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of disappointment. I stared at the screen, my jaw tight. It was a stark, unforgiving rejection letter. Three years of grinding, late nights fueled by instant coffee, all for this empty notification. I let out a slow, deliberate breath, the air catching in my chest. I picked up my pen, the plastic cool against my fingertips, and began to trace the lines of the rejection, my movements stiff and jerky. "Denied," I muttered, the word tasting like ash. I slammed the laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet dorm room. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small space like a caged animal, running a hand through my already messy hair. The sheer unfairness of it all made my hands clench into fists.

[story 2]

I reread the rejection three times, each time the disappointment deepening into a simmering frustration that burned just beneath my skin. My gaze drifted from the screen to the cluttered desk, at the stack of highlighted essays that now seemed utterly worthless. A low, frustrated sound escaped my throat—a tight, involuntary exhale. I shoved the papers aside with unnecessary force, the sound of the rustling paper grating on my nerves. "Seriously?" I whispered to the empty room, my voice laced with a brittle edge. I began to methodically gather my belongings, stuffing notebooks haphazardly into my backpack. Every movement was sharp, impatient. I felt a distinct tightening in my shoulders, a restless energy that wouldn't settle until I could do something about this injustice.

[story 3]

When I finally opened the official correspondence, the realization hit me like a physical blow. My shoulders slumped momentarily before snapping back into rigid tension. I picked up my phone, intending to call someone, but hesitated. The urge to lash out at the system, at the committee that had made this decision, was overwhelming. I paced to the window, watching the indifferent campus life unfold below. My knuckles were white where I gripped the phone. I let out a sharp, humorless laugh that died quickly in my throat. "Unbelievable," I said, the tone flat and devoid of warmth. I turned away from the window, the energy coiled inside me demanding release, a sharp, restless need to dismantle the situation piece by piece.
